B. Accomplishments/Planned Programs ($ in Millions)                                                                                      FY 2025    FY 2026        FY 2027
include testing eye protection and blunt force trauma capabilities transitioning from the Science and Technology community such
as anti-scratch coating, active light technology that detects laser threats, and improved blunt impact protection.
FY 2027 Plans:
Project will advance previously developed technologies and improved materials for Torso & Extremity Protection (TEP), Vital
Torso Protection (VTP), and the Integrated Head Protection System (IHPS) to reduce mass, increase protection, and align injury
criteria with validated test methods. Efforts will address defect limits and optimize lower performance thresholds for operational
application. The project will integrate soldier signature management capabilities to reduce multispectral detectability, enhance
soldier system integration and fitment to ensure compatibility and comfort across combat gear, and develop blast overpressure
mitigation features within torso and head protection systems. Test methods will be refined to measure protection against blast
overpressure, ballistic threats, performance across various operational environments, improve fitment, and evaluate new materials
for use against emerging threats, while advancing surveillance testing capabilities to update life cycle estimates, refine risk injury
criteria, and achieve continued mass reductions.

Technical efforts in developmental focus areas will evaluate current and future material and processing upgrades, conduct
technical testing on body armor designed to defeat multiple and emerging threats at lower weight, develop improved hard armor
ballistic performance, and integrating electronic technology into personal protective equipment to increase communication and
situational awareness capabilities while reducing Soldier load burden. Head protection work will include testing eye protection,
enhancing blunt force trauma capabilities, and transitioning technologies from the Science and Technology community such as
anti-scratch coatings, active light technology to detect laser threats, and integrated hearing protection systems to reduce auditory
injuries while maintaining situational awareness. These efforts will deliver improved protective performance, reduced Soldier load,
expanded protective coverage against blast and ballistic threats, and standardized fitment and integration criteria that enhance
survivability, comfort, and operational effectiveness across the force.
FY 2026 to FY 2027 Increase/Decrease Statement:
FY 2027 funding increase supports efforts to refine soldier signature management technology and improve total system
integration and fitment.
Title: Blast Overpressure Sensor System                                                                                                        -               -        3.000
Description: Blast Overpressure Sensor System (BOSS) will be a Soldier worn device that will track, store and transmit blast
overpressure events caused by a shock wave above normal atmospheric pressure. This shock wave may be caused by tier one
weapons or explosions in training scenarios and on the battlefield.

B. Accomplishments/Planned Programs ($ in Millions)                                                                                      FY 2025        FY 2026        FY 2027
The Blast Overpressure Sensor System (BOSS) project will address tactical integration, product development, data management,
medical record integration, and operational testing and evaluation.

The project will collaborate with the Science and Technology (S&T) community to assess existing medical technologies
and advance product development. The project will conduct research and development tests to evaluate the technology's
blast monitoring performance across various operational environments. The project scope includes in-depth tactical network
integration to transfer data from the sensor to the data repository and the medical record. The ultimate objective is to advance the
development of the BOSS for integration with the electronic health record, enhancing medical care for Warfighters exposed to
blast overpressure events.
FY 2026 to FY 2027 Increase/Decrease Statement:
FY 2027 funding increase supports advanced tactical integration and data repository support for future integration with the
electronic health record.

Congressional Add: Custom Biometric Wearables                                                                               -          7.500
FY 2026 Plans: This effort will evaluate custom in-ear, biometric hearing protection technology for future
integration with the Integrated Head Protection System (IHPS), communications equipment, and other hearing
protection devices. The effort will conduct research and development tests to measure the technology's steady
state noise attenuation, audio situational awareness, and impulse noise attenuation across various operational
environments. The project scope includes operational evaluations with ground and aviation personnel to gather
feedback regarding functional effectiveness, ergonomic fit, and biometric analysis. This project accelerates the
development of an advanced hearing protection system integrated with existing Personal Protective Equipment
(PPE) that increases Soldier survivability by preventing permanent and temporary hearing loss, delivering critical
biometric data to leadership, and maintaining mission-essential situational awareness.

D. Acquisition Strategy
The projects pursue technology transition from science and technology, and prototype development, culminating in the transition of mature technologies (Technology
Readiness Levels (TRL) 6-7) to Engineering and Manufacturing Development. This Project continues to exercise competitively awarded contracts using best value
source selection procedures where applicable.

A. Mission Description and Budget Item Justification
FD9: Robotic Systems improves the autonomous robotic systems portfolio by maturing cutting edge emergent commercial technology for transition, creating a virtual
test capability to support evaluation, developmental test, system safety testing and operational force-on-force execution for Concept of Operations (CONOP) and
Tactics, Techniques and Procedures (TTP) development, development of prototypes and to support infrastructure needs such as cloud-based tools for development
and deployment of Autonomy and Artificial Intelligence (AI) Machine Learning (ML) software and tools to support automated testing in a Development, Security and
Operations (DEVSECOPS) process. FD9 will also be used in support of developing Milestone Decision activities for emerging Programs.

A. Mission Description and Budget Item Justification
Robotics Systems funding will demonstrate new capabilities to meet current and future military needs and to determine integration potential across the Army Robotics
portfolio by testing and evaluating a variety of emerging technologies.

Robotics Systems (RS) improves robotic and autonomous program acquisition schedules by supporting the development of integrated and synchronized capability
documents and by maturing / transitioning technology. RDT&E funds enable support to capability development of emerging requirements. Activities include collaboration
with industry and government, studies, assessments, and document development such as Robotic open Interfaces, architectures, and specifications; Technology
Readiness Levels, Manufacturing Readiness Levels, Trade Studies, Letter of Sufficiency determinations, draft acquisition documents, and draft contract documents.

RS also supports the Army modernization strategy of the current Robotic fleets and current Army vehicles by investigating technology insertions including, but not limited
to condition-based maintenance, platform and payload electronics, autonomous operations and other ground robotics technologies.

FY 2027 funding will continue to demonstrate and explore technologies that support the Continuous Transformation Initiative published in 2025. Funding will also
support expansion of the virtual test and evaluation capabilities for autonomous software and electronic warfare.

B. Accomplishments/Planned Programs ($ in Millions)                                                                                          FY 2025            FY 2026       FY 2027

through M&S Software-in-the-loop (SITL) and Hardware-in-the-loop (HITL) allowing for transition into Program of Record.

This funding continues the development and integration for Armored and Infantry Formations. Autonomy is designed to enhance
Soldiers' decision-making capabilities by leveraging machines to provide additional information and reduce cognitive load. This
is achieved through the integration of ground robotic systems, key enablers, Unmanned Aerial Systems (UAS), and a variety
of payloads. This funding supports issuance of critical technologies for Soldier Touchpoints and experiments through software
productization, payload integration, training package finalization, and other related tasks. These activities are essential to mitigate

B. Accomplishments/Planned Programs ($ in Millions)                                                                                   FY 2025        FY 2026        FY 2027
Funding will be used to partner with industry and create standardized system of systems software for the next generation of
autonomous systems. Goals for FY 2026 are to reduce operator workload by developing software that lessens the cognitive
demands on users controlling robots and Modular Mission Payloads (MMPs). Funding will also improve command and control
with a focus on how information is shared and integrated.
FY 2027 Plans:
Funding will be used to partner with industry and create standardized system of systems software for the next generation of
autonomous systems. Goals for FY 2027 will focus on standards and interface development as well as a virtual sandbox for
simulation and experimentation, tools necessary to implement a buy-try-decide acquisition approach for Mission Autonomy
Common Control (MACC) software. Funding will also support fielding of initial MACC software to TiC units, and enhance software
capabilities being acquired by others, including the Pathway for Innovation and Transformation (PIT).
FY 2026 to FY 2027 Increase/Decrease Statement:
Decrease from FY 2026 to FY 2027 is due to the alignment of resources with the Army's product/feature roadmap for Advanced
Robotics.
                                                                                Accomplishments/Planned Programs Subtotals                  12.563      24.061          20.290

                                                                                                                     FY 2025    FY 2026
Congressional Add: Distributed Radio Frequency Photonic Systems                                                            -      12.500
FY 2026 Plans: Funding will support the Congressional add for Distributed Radio Frequency Photonic Systems
by improving battlefield sensing, sensor fusion, and resilient tactical communications in contested environments.
This effort will leverage advanced RF photonics and distributed fiber-based architectures to enable spectrally
diverse communications and sensing capabilities that operate outside the congested radio-frequency (RF)
spectrum. These technologies enable high-bandwidth, low-latency connectivity between distributed sensors,
command posts, and maneuver units, supporting real-time battlefield awareness and decision-making.

These capabilities support several operational benefits for Army formations:

* Improved distributed sensing and sensor fusion, enabling commanders to integrate data from geographically
dispersed sensors into a coherent operational picture.
* Resilient tactical communications, using photonic and fiber-based architectures that are significantly less
vulnerable to RF jamming and spectrum congestion.
                                                                                Congressional Adds Subtotals                -      12.500

C. Other Program Funding Summary ($ in Millions)
N/A
Remarks

D. Acquisition Strategy
Robotics Systems (RS) is designed to facilitate the transition of robotics and autonomous systems technology to capability set solutions. It informs the acquisition
process early in the development cycle allowing key stakeholders the ability to make integration decisions and affordability trades while writing requirements.

Efforts include Capabilities Document input, capturing technical and test data, close analysis of activities that feed cost estimates, provide test support, develop Modeling
and Simulation (M&S) capabilities, and develop a Software Integration Lab (SIL). Efforts will support Rapid Prototyping to inform emerging requirements and other
Army systems. A "buy/lease, try and inform" methodology may be used to evaluate Government Off the Shelf (GOTS), Commercial Off the Shelf (COTS), and Non-
Developmental Item (NDI) robotics products that have the potential to enhance Soldier combat effectiveness. Actual operational user feedback and evaluation results
obtained will inform emerging capabilities and requirements documents in support of a return on investment to support future Army decision making.

The Army will build and test prototype systems for safety release, Soldier use, and further technology maturation. Funds will be used to rapidly mature demonstrated
capabilities and to create initial training and maintenance documentation for insertion into capability sets.

Note
The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Expanded Mission Area Missile (EMAM) includes $58,189 thousand of discretionary and $5,000 thousand of mandatory
(reconciliation) for a total of $63,189 thousand.

A. Mission Description and Budget Item Justification
These funding lines are directly aligned to the Army Air and Missile Defense Modernization Priority. Work in this PE, the Expanded Mission Area Missile (EMAM)
program, supports the Integrated Air and Missile Defense (IAMD) architecture and provides Directed Energy Integrated Fires Protection Capability (DE-IFPC) systems
to defeat Cruise Missiles (CM); single and swarming Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS); Rocket, Artillery, and Mortar (RAM) threats; Fixed Wing (FW); and Rotary
Wing (RW) manned aircraft. This EMAM program element is made up of Joint Laser Weapon System - Army (JLWS-A), DE-IFPC, which is an Air Defense capability
consisting of the High Energy Laser (IFPC-HEL) and the High Power Microwave (IFPC-HPM), as well as the Multi-Domain Artillery Cannon System (MDACS).

Project B1A: JLWS is a partnered Army-Navy High Energy Laser (HEL) effort that will provide an Air Defense capability against cruise missile threats.

Project BU9: IFPC-HEL will provide a ground-based weapon system designed to acquire, track, engage, and defeat the CM, UAS, RAM, FW and RW threats. The IFPC-
HEL requirement consists of a standard military prime mover, high energy laser subsystem, power and thermal subsystem, and a beam control subsystem integrated
with battle management command, control and communication (BMC3) software. IFPC-HEL provides much needed protection against adversarial threat systems
capable of targeting U.S. and Allied forward operating bases and other critical assets. The IFPC-HEL prototype and data will be transferred to the Joint Laser Weapon
System (JLWS) program in FY 2027.

Project CO6: IFPC-HPM program consists of ground-based weapon systems designed to acquire, track, engage, and defeat single and swarming small-UAS. The IFPC-
HPM requirement consists of an HPM effector that is interoperable with BMC3 software. IFPC-HPM provides much needed protection against adversarial single and
swarming small-UAS in a semi-fixed position, while also growing capability to provide shoot-on-the-move protection of maneuver forces.

PE 0604019A: Expanded Mission Area Missile (EMAM)                                            UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                    Volume 2a - 291

Component Development & Prototypes (ACD&P)

Project DJ5: MDACS will provide a cannon based Air and Missile Defense (AMD) system to complement and integrate with existing AMD systems in the IAMD
architecture enabling cost-effective and flexible options to defend fixed and semi-fixed sites against attack by CM, UAS, and advanced threats and provide a capability
with a high rate of fire and a deep magazine to defeat threats in raid scenarios.

A. Mission Description and Budget Item Justification
This funding line is directly aligned to the Army Air and Missile Defense Modernization Priority.

The Directed Energy Integrated Fires Protection Capability (DE-IFPC) High Energy Laser (HEL) is an Air Defense capability for defense of fixed and semi-fixed sites
from Cruise Missiles (CM); Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS); Rocket, Artillery, and Mortar (RAM); Fixed Wing (FW); and Rotary Wing (RW) threats. This project
will deliver an operationally effective rapid prototype capability in FY 2026. IFPC-HEL funds an improved mechanism to effectively confront emerging threats and
advance the U.S. military dominance in accordance with the 2026 National Defense Strategy. Efforts include development, acquisition, test and evaluation, assessment,
maturation, and potential future transition of the prototype technology to an acquisition program.

B. Accomplishments/Planned Programs ($ in Millions)                                                                                     FY 2025          FY 2026       FY 2027
Title: IFPC-High Energy Laser                                                                                                              18.774           16.416            -
Description: This effort will provide planning, prototype manufacturing, and testing for the IFPC-HEL prototype with a planned
transition to support future laser weapon programs. The IFPC-HEL is a modularized laser weapon system that can be integrated
on a standard military prime mover to defend fixed and semi-fixed sites from CM, UAS, RAM, FW and RW threats.

FY 2026 Plans:
FY 2026 funds will support final prototype test and integration and assessment for delivery in FY 2026 and begin CONUS
Contractor Logistic Support (CLS). FY 2026 funds will ensure CLS coverage into FY 2027 to transfer the prototype system and
data into the Joint Laser Weapon System (JLWS) program to support continued lethality testing against joint threats.
FY 2026 to FY 2027 Increase/Decrease Statement:
The decrease from FY 2026 to FY 2027 reflects the transfer of IFPC-HEL prototype to the JLWS program.
                                                                                  Accomplishments/Planned Programs Subtotals                18.774         16.416                -

D. Acquisition Strategy
The IFPC-HEL prototype weapon system will be delivered in FY 2026 and demonstrate directed energy lethality against joint threat sets to continue to inform future laser
weapon programs. Continuous soldier integration will be conducted to provide feedback in support of Army requirements generation/soldier centered design, prototype
maturation, fielding, and future capability development. Performance characteristics measured in test, evaluation and assessment will inform future acquisition activities.

Microwave (HPM)
Quantity of RDT&E Articles                   -         -          -           -           -            -         -           -          -            -

A. Mission Description and Budget Item Justification
This funding line is directly aligned to the Army Air and Missile Defense Modernization Priority.

The Directed Energy Integrated Fires Protection Capability (DE-IFPC) High Power Microwave (HPM) is a Counter-Unmanned Aircraft Systems (C-UAS) capability
consisting of HPM prototypes with residual combat capability in support of Multi-Domain Operations (MDO). The IFPC-HPM program provides the Army with HPM
prototype weapon systems of various designs for short-range defense against single Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) and swarms. IFPC-HPM funds an improved
mechanism to effectively confront emerging threats and advance the United States' military dominance in accordance with the 2025 Interim National Defense Strategy.
Efforts include development, acquisition, test and evaluation, assessment, maturation, and future transition of prototype technologies to an acquisition program.

B. Accomplishments/Planned Programs ($ in Millions)                                                                                    FY 2025           FY 2026       FY 2027
Title: IFPC-High Power Microwave                                                                                                           3.884            11.773            -
Description: This effort will provide development, planning, prototype manufacturing, testing and evaluation of four (4) Generation
1 HPMs, two (2) Generation 2 HPMs, and up to four (4) mobile HPM rapid prototypes with residual combat capability. The IFPC-
HPM is a weapon system of various designs that provides short range defense against Group 1-2 UAS and swarms. IFPC-HPM
will increase commonality through cross-program open standard sharing within Army PAE Fires and across other Services. IFPC-
HPM leverages previous HPM technology demonstrations and facilitates continued assessment.

FY 2026 Plans:
FY 2026 funds will support evaluation, demonstration and assessment for various HPM systems; to include the addition of up
to four (4) systems of various designs; continue CONUS/OCONUS CLS; and prepare for a potential transition of prototype
technologies to a formal acquisition program to include SEPM and associated program effort advancing directed energy.
FY 2026 to FY 2027 Increase/Decrease Statement:
FY 2027 program decrease to fund Army priority efforts.

C. Other Program Funding Summary ($ in Millions)
N/A
Remarks

D. Acquisition Strategy
IFPC-HPM will utilize streamlined acquisition methods, processes and techniques to rapidly prototype the capability. The Rapid Capabilities and Critical Technologies
Office (RCCTO) awarded a Prototype Other Transaction Agreement (pOTA) which delivered four (4) Generation 1 HPM prototype systems in FY 2024, delivered two
(2) Generation 2 HPM prototypes systems in FY 2026, and will deliver up to four (4) mobile HPM rapid prototypes to soldiers in FY 2027. Continuous Soldier Integration
(CSI) events will be conducted to provide feedback in support of Army requirements generation, prototype maturation, fielding residual combat capability to a unit of
action, and potential future capability development.

DJ5: Multi-Domain Artillery                 -     30.000         -     235.593           -       235.593    361.144      241.348     126.001     216.175          0.000    1,210.261
Cannon System (MDACS)
Quantity of RDT&E Articles                  -         -          -          -            -            -          -            -           -            -

Note
The Cannon Based Air Defense System (CBADS) is within the Expanded Mission Area Missile (EMAM) program in FY 2026. CBADS was formerly submitted under the
name MDACS (Multi-Domain Artillery Cannon System).

The program received $5 million of mandatory funding in FY 2026.

A. Mission Description and Budget Item Justification
The Cannon Based Air Defense System (CBADS) mission is to defend Joint Force fixed and semi-fixed locations against attack by a broad spectrum of Unmanned
Aerial Systems (UASs), Cruise Missiles (CMs), Fixed Wing (FW), Rotary Wing (RW), and other advanced air and missile threats and to complement existing air and
missile defenses by operating in integrated or stand-alone operational scenarios. Additionally, CBADS may provide a future surface-to-surface long-range precision fires
capability.

CBADS is a rapid prototype and Integrated Air and Missile Defense (IAMD) Battle Command System (IBCS) integrated system consisting of a Multi-Domain Artillery
Cannon (MDAC), Multi-Function Precision Radar (MFPR), Multi-Domain Battle Manager (MDBM), Hypervelocity Projectiles (HVP), and ammunition handling vehicles.

CBADS will build onto the Army Integrated Air and Missile Defense (AIAMD) open systems architecture allowing seamless sensor and shooter connectivity for a layered
defense with a mission command structure integrated into the IAMD Battle Command System (IBCS). CBADS will increase battlefield flexibility and raid capacity,
resulting in increased AMD effectiveness and efficiency and reduced attrition of advanced interceptor missiles in the Army's inventory.

The Army will leverage current Office of the Secretary of War (OSW) Strategic Capabilities Office (SCO) investments in the Hypervelocity Gun Weapon System (HGWS)
to develop an Army design tailored to address Army requirements and assess CBADS at a Battery level in FY 2030. The prototype will inform an enduring capability
requirement and decision in late FY 2026. The outcome of the decision review will be to determine whether to build a battery prototype for fielding or to initiate a
traditional acquisition program for CBADS to meet the FY 2030 fielding date.
.

B. Accomplishments/Planned Programs ($ in Millions)                                                                                       FY 2025          FY 2026        FY 2027
Title: Multi Domain Artillery Cannon System (MDACS)                                                                                          30.000               -         235.593



PE 0604019A: Expanded Mission Area Missile (EMAM)                         UNCLASSIFIED

Appropriation/Budget Activity                                                  R-1 Program Element (Number/Name)            Project (Number/Name)
2040 / 4                                                                       PE 0604019A / Expanded Mission Area Mi       DJ5 / Multi-Domain Artillery Cannon System
                                                                               ssile (EMAM)                                 (MDACS)

B. Accomplishments/Planned Programs ($ in Millions)                                                                                 FY 2025     FY 2026       FY 2027
Description: This effort will leverage SCO investments in HGWS but develop an Army design tailored to address Army
requirements, manufacture the prototypes, integrate and test the System of Systems, and conduct an Operational Assessment
(OA) of the Battery formation NLT FY 2030, providing a residual combat capability.

The CBADS prototype Battery delivered to the Army post-Operational Assessment consists of up to:
1) Eight (8) Multi-Domain Artillery Cannons (MDAC) - 155 mm / 58 caliber automated cannon systems mounted onto a wheeled
tactical vehicle
2) Four (4) Multifunction Precision Radars (MFPR) - High precision multifunctioning radar mounted onto a wheeled tactical vehicle
3) Two (2) Multi-Domain Battle Managers (MDBM) - Fire Control and Command & Control (C2) center mounted onto a wheeled
tactical vehicle
4) One hundred and forty-four (144) HVPs - Radar guided, cannon-fire projectiles
5) Eight (8) ammunition handling vehicles

FY 2027 Plans:
FY 2027 funds will be used to develop a baseline architecture, allocate subsystem requirements, and initiate CBAD prime item
final designs. All remaining long lead material purchases will be placed on contract. Component and subsystem Contractor
Verification Testing will be initiated. Software algorithm development will also be initiated. Additionally, testing consumables
necessary for cannon and projectile development and safety testing will be purchased or fabricated and developmental and safety
test planning initiated. Additionally, continue Integrated Battle Command System (IBCS) integration, test and verify subsystem
software and performance against threats through the use of Hardware-in-the-Loop (HWIL) and Modeling and Simulations (M&S).
FY 2026 to FY 2027 Increase/Decrease Statement:
FY 2027 funding increase will be used for long lead material purchases and testing of consumables necessary for cannon and
projectile development and safety testing.
                                                                              Accomplishments/Planned Programs Subtotals              30.000              -      235.593

D. Acquisition Strategy
The Army is waiting on FY 2026 OSW SCO HGWS development outcomes prior to making decisions on system architecture design, acquisition pathway, and
acquisition strategy. The program is posturing for entrance into a Middle Tier of Acquisition for Rapid Prototyping (MTA-RP) pathway.

The CBADS requirement document, an Abbreviated Capability Development Document (A-CDD) is on pause in the Army Requirements Oversight Council (AROC)
staffing process pending outcomes of the FY 2026 OSW SCO HGWS development and test outcomes as well as TRADOC Requirements Analysis Center - White
Sands Missile Range (TRAC-WSMR) analysis on cannon-based air defense schedule to conclude 3QFY26.

The program will employ contracted sustainment services through an operational assessment. Should the capability transition to an Middle Tier Acquisition - Rapid
Fielding (MTA-RF) the project office would develop an enduring sustainment strategy.

Throughout the duration of the Portfolio Acquisition Executive (PAE) Defensive Fires (formally RCCTO) CBADS effort, Contractor Logistics Support (CLS) will be
provided to maintain and sustain CBADS, ensuring the system remains operational and effective. Upon formal transition of the CBADS program to sustainment partners,
each office will provide product support for and manage sustainment of their CBADS subsystem components, maximizing remote logistics where possible to maintain
and modernize subsystems to improve efficiency and combat readiness.
